Um, so everyone recalls January, there was a surge in federal, immigration enforcement presence in, in Maine, and there was a resulting economic disruption for some households, either, folks, not feeling they could leave their house, go to work, or businesses that closed

**[49:02]** because workers weren't showing up, or, whatever reason. So the mayor had asked for, based on some public feedback, that we received and checking with some councilors that, we have a potential... We have a workshop on potential rental assistance, for, households that are impacted. And so the goal was to prevent housing instability and evictions that may occur, without such assistance. So the key questions ultimately for the council, and I, I will display these at the end too, is should the city provide financial assistance to residents experiencing economic disruption? And if the answer is yes, how should that assistance be delivered? So we'll talk about whether it's run through the city or if it's via a donation to a nonprofit to administer.

**[49:47]** Uh, and then of course, what should the total funding amount be? Um, and if it's city administered, should the council adopt the staff proposed program structure? We took a little time working with our social services director to, to, outline what a program would look like if, city run. So option one for funding to a nonprofit, the city would allocate funds, to a nonprofit organization that would administer the rental assistance. We can attach some conditions to it. For example, only assisting South Portland households. Uh, the nonprofit would then be responsible for taking in the applications, determining whether someone's eligible, and then they would distribute funds, to landlords or applicants. You could require it that it's, given to landlords and not

**[50:32]** applicants, if that's, a concern, which understandably it could be. Uh, there's a couple potential partners that I've identified here for you, Project HOME, which is run by the Quality Housing Coalition and the Cumberland County Homeless Prevention Program run through Opportunity Alliance. So those are two examples of, of, entities the city could partner with or provide that funding to. The pros and cons there, the pros are that nonprofits already have an established relationship and trust in impacted communities. Um, they're, they're in some cases already doing this work. It reduces administrative workload that would fall on city staff if we were to administer it. And nonprofits may provide additional outreach to residents in need that we frankly, might not be able to. The cons, of course, is there's less direct oversight by the city, although we're

**[51:20]** sending money, for this purpose, perhaps with some strings. Um, you know, we're not, deciding cases on an individual basis. There may be potential administrative fees, but I'm not aware that the two, groups that I mentioned would charge those. Um, and there may be some desire for the city to still monitor reporting and compliance if there are kind of, conditions or, or strings attached to those dollars. The second option would be a city administered program. It would be, ultimately administered through our social services general assistant department. Uh, the process would be very similar. Applicants submit documentation of income disruption, which we can talk about in a little bit. Um, if we verify that there's housing instability, then we would provide assistance and pay it directly to, as we've

**[52:06]** proposed it, landlords. Uh, some of the requirements, and again, these are things that we've come up with, and if you do choose to have this be city administered, if this is something you wanna do, we'd want you to weigh in on some of these things. But certainly you have to be a resident of South Portland and occupy a rental unit within the city. You need to demonstrate that you have responsibility for paying rent, so you're not someone that's sleeping on the couch and not responsible for the rent. You're the actual person that, is responsible on the lease. Uh, you demonstrate that you had an income loss or employment disruption between January 15th, which was, a little bit before the surge happened, but rumors were certainly swirling, and then March 1st, that covers a nice long timeframe, that could be associated with economic disruption related to federal enforcement activity. Uh, demonstrate housing instability, such as your

**[52:55]** rent is in arrears, you've received late notices or an ev- or at eviction risk. Um, we would recommend the person first apply for general assistance or seek other sources of funding. We've actually had several folks inquire about possible assistance, and we've run them either through GA or, we've referred them to other agencies, and they've been able to find, the resources that they've needed. Uh, and so if those have been exhausted, this would almost be like a program of, of last resort. Uh, we propose limiting it to two months of assistance, a maximum of three thousand per household, and, and again, paid directly to the landlord. Uh, we would accept applications until June 1st or when funds are exhausted, whichever comes first. Any sort of funds that were unused would come back to the source.

**[53:41]** So for example, if you pulled this from your fund balance, it would go back to fund balance. Uh, and then also importantly, we would not require the disclosure of immigration status, as part of any application, nor would we voluntarily share any of this program information or applications or otherwise with federal enforcement authorities unless we, of course, required to by law or some sort of court order or warrant. Pros and cons here, there's very strong oversight and accountability of municipal funds. Um, the assistance is clearly targeted towards, this situation and housing stabilization, and there's consistent eligibility standards and verification procedures that we would have in place. The cons, again, requires administrative capacity from our existing city staff. There's no new staff that would be,

**[54:29]** accepting and reviewing, this and, and cutting the checks to landlords. The purpose of this presentation is to bring forward a bike share ordinance framework and to assess City Council's interest in bringing a bike share program to South Portland. They go hand in hand. So in this, presentation, we're gonna provide some background information on bike share, key findings from a public survey that we ran, a proposed framework for this

**[109:15]** bike share ordinance, as well as a brief overview of what a bike share program in South Portland might look like, to help inform your deliberations. And then finally, we have a few questions, to, guide the, the, the input that we receive from council.So, a little bit of background on why we are bringing this forward. So first, the motivation for exploring a bike share reflects goals included in the One Climate Future plan. Um, approximately thirty percent of South Portland's greenhouse gas emissions come from the transportation sector. And a key milestone in the

**[110:01]** plan is that five percent of all trips should be completed by bike by twenty forty. And the plan also speaks to the need to revisit efforts to launch a bike share program in the cities of, Portland and South Portland. Portland has already established theirs, South Portland obviously is still considering this. So evidence, that is based on the experience of small, medium, and large cities, shows that bike share can benefit-- generate a range of benefits. Um, this includes, increasing the percentage of people who, commute by bike. Um, a nationwide study showed that when both transit and bike

**[110:47]** share are available, fifty percent of users used both. And additionally, all of this can translate to really substantial reductions in greenhouse gas emissions. So there are two primary reasons that we think, it's prudent to consider moving a bike share program into the transportation mix in South Portland. First is to facilitate a mode shift away from car trips to bike trips, and the second is to increase equitable access to resilient transportation options. So d-- reduced car dependency will reduce carbon emissions, and bike share can help fill those first and last mile connectivity

**[111:35]** gaps so that people can, better access public transit if they're not close to a bus stop or, if they arrive at a bus stop and they just need to go that last little bit. A key consideration for our bike share program, would be thinking about interoperability with Portland's bike share system. So for example, we heard the example of, it being difficult to get to Portland from SMCC or other parts of South Portland. Um, so if a student at SMCC, has an internship in another part of the city or needs to get across the river, bike share might help them do that more effectively. Um, experience also shows that, when people engage in using bike share, that extends to their bike use outside

**[112:23]** of bike share. So, it, it's building a bike culture, and people use bikes more frequently outside of bike share with their personal bikes. And we know that with more cyclists on the road, that increases visibility, it makes the road safer for everybody. So the second piece is that bike share can lower the bar to entry for riding a bike. Um, it can be more convenient and cost-effective for those residents who might not be able to invest in a bike, or it's difficult for them to maintain their own bike or store it. Storage can be a real problem. Um, bike share can also attract new bike riders. Um, it's a low commitment way of testing it out. Do I like biking around? Um, is this something I wanna do in the future more?

**[113:10]** And then I can make an investment. And finally, bike share can contribute to transportation resilience. Um, so if transit isn't reaching the places that people need to go, this might help fill that gap, while promoting sustainability, equity, and connectivity. So again, a bike share program we feel is an investment in South Portland's future, addressing transportation challenges while promoting those pieces of sustainability, equity, and connectivity. So to gather broader input on a bike share program, we conducted a public survey. And the survey was open for two months, between November twenty-eighth and January

**[113:57]** thirtieth. Uh, we sent out, mail surveys to, a random sample of seven hundred and fifty addresses in South Portland. Um, we used city newsletters and social media, which had a digital survey link. We had, mailing lists and, the su-sustainability office social media. We had flyers and handouts at key locations and events throughout the city that also had the digital survey link. And we also had, physical paper surveys available at City Hall so that people could fill them out and drop them in the box. And we received responses from every neighborhood in the city. So when we asked a key question of, "How interested would

**[114:43]** you be in having a bike share program in South Portland?" Um, fifty-two percent of residents indicated that they would be either very or somewhat interested in having a bike share program in South Portland. Um, thirty-three percent of respondents said they were not interested or, not interested at all, and fifteen percent of residents were neutral. Notably, forty-one percent of respondents who currently-- who don't currently own a bike, 'cause we did ask that question, were interested in a bike share program. So forty-one percent of folks who don't have a bike already are interested in bike share. Among, the residents who are interested in bike

**[115:29]** share, fifty-three percent said they would like to use bike share for multiple purposes. Um, so running errands was the most frequent response, with recreation and exercise being the next most frequentUm, but it's interesting that the majority of the recreation and exercise responses, because this was a, a select all that apply, seventy percent of those folks indicated some other use that they would like to combine with recreation and exercise. Um, commuting and connecting to transit were less frequent responses. Um, so when we asked folks if they had concerns about a bike share in South Portland, lack of bike infrastructure and

**[116:16]** traffic concerns were by far the most frequent responses. These se-received almost, double, any of the other responses. So that really stood out as something that people were worried about. Uh, user skill, cost, helmet availability, and station locations all received roughly in the vicinity of a hundred responses each. Um, and this was a select all question, so people could choose multiple of these. We also asked an open-ended question at the end of the survey, and, people provided their input, and we grouped these into some of the, the major things we were hearing. So first was that people were really, asking

**[117:02]** for a concurrent investment in transit systems. So this pairs really nicely with the discussion with Metro tonight, but people wanted to be able to connect these two things. The second was that, people wanted a regional connectivity. So that interoperability with Portland, for example, being able to do a one-way trip from South Portland to downtown Portland without having to park their car, was something that people really thought would be valuable. Um, people reiterated their strong concern about safety. Um, they wanted bike lanes and less dangerous road design, particularly on Broadway, Westbrook, and in the mall area. There was also a desire for training. Um, this is both for the cyclists who would use the bike share program, but

**[117:49]** also for drivers. Um, they're on both sides of that equation. Um, just safer, safer road behavior, for both cyclists and drivers. And then finally, there was concern about the potential cost of a program and questions about whether, this is, the best inve-investment relative to other things that we could be putting money toward. And this was mentioned in a little less than ten percent of the open-ended responses. So, the key survey takeaways are, we did learn that a majority of respondents are interested in a bike share program. Um, and

**[118:35]** that bike share can serve multiple needs and purposes in our city. Um, notably, many residents own bikes, but are not riding. Um, and that, that has a lot to do with those concerns about road safety, and traffic concerns. Um, and so those are things we need to be looking at in tandem. So any efforts to develop a bike share program, should be coupled with improvements to bike infrastructure and education. And in some good news, the city has received a community action grant, to move forward on developing a citywide, bike ped master plan. And we've also been taking concrete steps, like the launch of the Cash Thornton Neighborhood Byway that provides a

**[119:22]** safe route for our middle schoolers to bike or walk to school. And we also have our Complete Streets policy and Complete Streets design manual in place. So, I think we are already taking meaningful steps on those infrastructure questions, and, and hopefully those improvements will be realized, alongside any potential bike share program. Okay, so getting into the framework, we, incorporated our information gathering efforts as well as guidelines developed by the National Association of City Transportation Officials, or NACTO. So if you hear me say NACTO, it's referring to that organization that developed these guidelines. Um, the framework that we're putting forward,

**[120:09]** has three areas: general terms and conditions, general operations, and system design. So under general terms and conditions, we propose including just a basic definition of a bike share system. So what are we regulating? Um, and building off of the City of Portland's, adapting that to our context, it could be something like a system of bicycles or electric bicycles that is made available for rent for general public use, generally for short periods of time, to include docks for storing bicycles when not in use. Um, so that's the kind of, parameters that we would be setting on what we're regulating. We also recommend including a provision that any bike share operator

**[120:55]** would have to seek legal permission via a license, permit, or contract, or some combination thereof, to operate. Um, we recommend that those be renewed annually, and that just means that if, the operator is not complying, with the, the agreement, we have the, ability to, not renew that in the future. Um, we also recommend that the city, require the bike share operator, to provide indemnity and hold appropriate insurance. Um, there's also a provision to clarify that the bike share operator will be responsible for any additional costs that might stem from bike share. So that might be increased repair or maintenance

**[121:40]** costs that stem from having bike share in the city.Second, under general conditions, we would like the ordinance to spell out safety and maintenance requirements. Um, so first, the operator should comply with local, state, and federal safety standards. For example, the type of equipment that's used should meet those bas-basic standards. Um, it's also possible to set a maximum e-bike speed. So for example, NACTO recommends a maximum speed of twenty miles per hour, for a bike share program. Um, but council could s-consider something lower, for example, fifteen miles per hour if they felt the twenty miles per hour was, not

**[122:26]** appropriate. Finally, there's the possibility to limit the number of bikes in the system or define how regularly these should be rebalanced, 'cause a bike share system, you occasionally need to move the bikes around to make sure the bikes are available at all stations at all times. With regard to maintenance, NACTO recommends full monthly checks, and weekly street checks. Uh, we believe that more frequent checks for e-bikes would be wise, and this could be on the order of every one to three days. Um, the ordinance should also specify what records need to be kept of that maintenance and how they should be submitted to the city so that we can make sure that the bike operator is doing what they said they were going to do.

**[123:13]** So we also recommend that the City of South Portland reserve the right to approve key aspects of the system design. In some cities, they leave it up to the, the bike share operator to make those decisions, but we think it would be important for South Portland to have control over those aspects. Um, so this can include, pricing and payment options, for example, a monthly or annual membership that could reduce the cost for frequent riders and cash payment options, to reduce access barriers. Um, notably, we really want to ensure that the pricing is affordable for short trips while being less cost effective for longer trips,

**[123:58]** and to reduce competitions with, businesses that might, provide bike rentals on a longer duration basis. We also strongly recommend, allowing only bikes that are docked at a fixed station. Some bike shares allow you to park, wherever you arrive. Um, we do not recommend this. Um, we've had discussions with our Parks and Recreation department and Public Works director, and so, these will be docked stations, i-in areas that are away from sidewalks, but in the public right of way. Um, we also recommend the inclusion of e-bikes, and the reason for this is that it extends the range that people can go using the bike share system. Um, but we want a mix potentially of these conventional, pedal bikes and e-bikes.

**[124:46]** Um, for example, Portland has a fifty-fifty mix. Um, and finally, we think that the ordinance should allow South Portland, to approve the station locations. We wanna make sure that these are in locations that are going to be close to important services that our residents need to access. Okay, so what, what would a potential bike share program look like in South Portland? I'm just gonna provide a high-level overview, so you can envision how it might work. So first of all, we think that there are five key principles that should guide any potential program that might come to fruition. This is, we want it to be accessible, affordable, safe, transit-oriented, and reliable. Um, and in the following slide, we just

**[125:35]** talk a little bit about what that would look like in practice. So, in terms of operations, the operator will be responsible for launching and managing the bike fleets and stations on a day-to-day basis. Um, that includes, payments, as well as, the installation of the stations in the spring and the removal in the fall because they get removed over the winter season. Um, we propose that the bike share start as a one-year pilot with forty bikes across eight stations with twenty conventional and twenty e-assist. And again, this, this notion of requiring the operator to be inter-interoperable with the Portland bike share we think is a really important piece.

**[126:23]** In terms of safety, we would like to require all users, and indeed, most bike share operators would require this, to read and sign an agreement, to follow the rules of the road, and to strongly encourage helmet use. Um, as a side note, adults over the age of sixteen are not required to wear bike helmets in Maine, and bike share operators typically don't provide them, because of logistical and sanitary reasons. Um, but there are options that we can pursue for increasing access to helmets, such as partnering with the Bicycle Coalition of Maine to do helmet giveaways, in the spring when people are starting up their bike season, et cetera. Um, we also wanna have educational signage at each of the bike stations, as, as a, an additional reminder

**[127:12]** of how to bike safely, and, what the rules of the road are. For maintenance, the operator will be responsible for the day-to-day monitoring of the, the bikes themselves as well as the stations. But there are two options that have been presented to us, as, possibilities for performing maintenance. The first is that the bike share hires a local field technician who would, conduct that maintenance on a regular basis. Um, there's also the possibility that the bike share operator could contract with a local bike repair shop who would perform the needed maintenance.Okay, so, funding. Um, this is an, an important question. Um, the establishment of a bike share

**[127:59]** program in South Portland was identified as a high priority by MaineDOT in their three-year work plan. Uh, based on this, we anticipate one hundred thousand dollars of, state funding being available in 2026, pending council's interest in pursuing the program. However, this would require a twenty, twenty thousand dollar match in the FY27 CIP to unlock this funding, and there would be no funding obligation beyond year one. Um, if the program continues into the future, there are three funding sources which, based on an estimate from Tandem Mobility, which runs Portland's bike share program, would include, twenty percent user fees

**[128:45]** and memberships, sixty percent sponsorships and advertising revenue, and, twenty percent grants, or municipal budget. **[170:09]** So, are we, Councilor West, would you like to go first? Um, sure. Are you interested in pursuing a bike share program? My answer is no. It's, it falls on their shoulders. And we all know that our Public Works Department's already working at capacity. I'm very proud of what they do, but they do a lot with a lean staff. **[170:56]** actually took some photos of the bikes in the, in the, creating the hazards to the pedestrians, where people just leave them and all that stuff. Then the second reason that I think we have to really look at seriously tonight, South Portland just does not have the right design as a city for a bike share program to be effective. It's too spread out. And an indication of that is that this bike share p- these bike share entities want us to pay. $100,000 plus $20,000 to get us eight stations with 40 bikes is $3,000 a bicycle. And when you look at the cities where bike shares are successful, it's a

**[171:43]** revenue generator for the city. I wrote down a couple of the ones. In, in Boise, Idaho, under the recent agreement, they, the bike share company pays an annual contract fee of $100,000 along with 10 cents a trip. The company also pays $106 fee for every device that needs to be moved or removed dur- due to a complaint. In Baltimore, a permit lasts for one year and costs $70,000. The companies pay 10 cents per ride per vehicle and hun- and $10,000 each year in performance bonds for damage and all that stuff. And there's also other kinds of charges. In, in, Oakland, California, they an- they pay a non-refundable permit fee of $2,500

**[172:30]** plus an annual $30,000 fee, a vehicle fee of $64 per bicycle per year, and then all kinds of other fees for the abandonment and everything else. Where a bike... And w- and for us, with as tight as we are, I would just urge us to do everything we can to get that $100,000 reallocated for hardscape.
